item.title: What even is food authenticity? Why we guard carbonara — and flatten chicken rice.
item.description: There is a peculiar theater to the internet’s culinary landscape, and if you spend enough time observing it, you will inevitably stumble into a warzone over authenticity. For example, no action incites internet fury faster than recipes of spaghetti carbonara that includes a splash of cream (or, god forbid …
item.dc:creator: Josh Izaac

item.title: Building a vintage recipe rolodex with Python, Pelican, and Markdown.
item.description:
I enjoy cooking. Not only that, but I enjoy complicated cooking — which can sometimes be a bit of a curse.

A simple weeknight meal? Not interested. A week-long project to nail the ultimate Xiao Long Bao, Croissants, or homemade ramen? Sign me up! I become somewhat obsessive, reading cooking blogs …
item.dc:creator: Josh Izaac

item.title: What are quantum computers, and how can we train them in Python?
item.description: Earlier this year, I was lucky enough to give a talk on quantum computing and near-term quantum machine learning at PyCon 2021. I’ve always loved the PyCon and SciPy conferences; in fact, one of my earliest memories during the first month of my quantum computing job was watching A …
item.dc:creator: Josh Izaac

item.title: Recursive custom gradients in TensorFlow
item.description: Most autodifferentiation libraries, such as PyTorch, TensorFlow, Autograd — and even PennyLane in the quantum case — allow you to create new functions and register custom gradients that the autodiff framework makes use of during backpropagation. This is useful in several cases; perhaps you have a composite function with a gradient that …
item.dc:creator: Josh Izaac
